challenges.title: Daily Art #2 — Mother and baby or young child

options.name: 'The Joy of Motherhood'
options.exhibitScript: undefined

options.name: Interior with a Mother Combing her Child’s Hair
options.dimensions: height 44.5 cm x width 38 cm x height 61 cm x width 53.5 cm x thickness 4.7 cm
options.exhibitScript: These two children serve as examples of good and bad upbringing. Toys litter the floor in a dimly lit bedroom. A mother combs the long curls of her son, who obediently leans on her lap. Meanwhile, her daughter mischievously sticks out her tongue in the mirror – is it directed at her reflection or at the maid in the background?

options.name: The Housewife
options.exhibitScript: undefined

answer.name: Gezicht op Algiers met Michiel Adriaenszoon de Ruyters schip 'De Liefde', 1662
answer.dimensions: support: height 65.5 cm x width 110 cm
answer.exhibitScript: Four views of North-African cities. Reinier Nooms, c. 1665. To protect the Dutch merchant fleet, the navy took the fight the enemy, far beyond the country's coastal waters. The Mediterranean harbours shown here - Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Salee - were bases from which privateers attacked Dutch merchant shipping. Attempts were made to impose treaties on these cities using the threat of naval force. Between 1661 and 1663 Admiral Michiel de Ruyter cruised the Mediterranean Sea in an effort to stamp out piracy. De Ruyter's ship, de Liefde, is shown in the foreground of the view of Algiers. This series of paintings was displayed in one of the rooms of the Amsterdam Admiralty. The Republic's navy was divided into five regional admiralties, one of which was based in Amsterdam.

options.name: The windmill at Wijk bij Duurstede
options.exhibitScript: The windmill rises up majestically, defying the dark rain clouds and overshadowing the castle and the church of Wijk bij Duurstede. The River Lek flows in the foreground. This painting is world famous, and rightly so. In this impressive composition, Ruisdael united all the typical Dutch elements – the low-lying land, the water and the expansive sky – manipulating them to converge on the equally characteristic Dutch mill.

options.name: The Caulking of Ships at the Bothuisje on Het IJ in Amsterdam
options.exhibitScript: Caulking the ships on the IJ at Amsterdam by Reinier Nooms, mid 17th century. Wooden sailing ships needed constant maintenance. They had to be examined after every voyage. To close gaps in the ship's outer planking with pitch and old rope(caulking), the vessel was careened on to its side bringing the hull out of water. Big ships only came into the harbour if they were due for repairs or to be laid up. Otherwise, they would remain out in the open water: in front of the line of posts (left) that protected the city and harbour from the IJ. The cargo was transported to and from the quay in smaller boats.

options.name: Schepen op een onstuimige zee

answer.name: Farmstead with the Gentleman Farmer and his Wife and the Painter in the foreground

options.name: Italian Landscape with Girl milking a Goat
options.exhibitScript:
These Italian peasants have risen early to milk the, cattle. A soft yellow mist still hangs in the valley. Du Jardin underscored their simple life by portraying the woman with bare feet. Clad in vibrant blue and red clothes, the two figures stand out brightly against
the yellow-brown mountain landscape.

options.name: De melkbocht
options.exhibitScript: undefined

answer.name: Aernout van Citters (1633-96). Ambassadeur van de Verenigde Nederlanden te Londen

options.name: Cornelis Calkoen on his Way to his Audience with Sultan Ahmed III
options.exhibitScript: Calkoen’s audience with Sultan Ahmed III took place on 14 September 1727 and followed a strict protocol. Here we see the retinue, unarmed according to convention, enter-ing the second courtyard of Topkapı Palace. Audiences were always held on the day that the Janissaries, the elite fighting force, received their pay. Just as Calkoen enters, the soldiers lunge noisily at the dishes of rice.

options.name: Portrait of a Couple in a Landscape, probably Isaac Abrahamsz Massa (1586-1643) and Beatrix van der Laen (1592-1639)
options.dimensions: support: height 140 cm x width 166.5 cm
options.exhibitScript: It was rare for a married couple to be portrayed lifesize and together in a single painting, and even more so in such an intimate and joyful manner. The patron would have had to have been receptive to such innovation. The close friendship between Hals and the newlywed couple, presumably Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, likely played a crucial role in facilitating the creation of this audacious portrait.

options.name: Giuliano & Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo Piero di Cosimo (1462–1522)
options.exhibitScript: In these pendant portraits, the Florentine architect Giuliano da Sangallo is portrayed with his recently deceased father Francesco, who was a musician as well as an architect. These two panels are among the earliest examples of portraits in which the sitter’s vocation is central. The quill and compass denote architecture, the sheet of notes refers to music: both arts are based on harmony and proportion.

options.name: Self Portrait
options.exhibitScript: Vincent moved to Paris in 1886, after hearing from his brother Theo about the new, colourful style of French painting. Wasting no time, he tried it out in several self-portraits. He did this mostly to avoid having to pay for a model. Using rhythmic brushstrokes in striking colours, he portrayed himself here as a fashionably dressed Parisian.

answer.name: Landscape with Hermit, Pilgrim and Peasant Woman

options.name: Gerard Andriesz Bicker (1622-66), Lord of Engelenburg. High Bailiff of Muiden
options.exhibitScript: Like his father, the twenty-year-old Gerard Bicker is portrayed as self-assured, his arm akimbo. The striking differences in the garments worn by father and son confirm that they are from different generations. While his father Andries is dressed in dignified black clothing with an old-fashioned ruff, Gerard wears a colourful and showy outfit with a flat collar and elegant gloves. Gerard was not awarded as many key administrative positions in Amsterdam.

options.name: Giuliano & Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo Piero di Cosimo (1462–1522)
options.exhibitScript: In these pendant portraits, the Florentine architect Giuliano da Sangallo is portrayed with his recently deceased father Francesco, who was a musician as well as an architect. These two panels are among the earliest examples of portraits in which the sitter’s vocation is central. The quill and compass denote architecture, the sheet of notes refers to music: both arts are based on harmony and proportion.

options.name: Self Portrait
options.exhibitScript: Vincent moved to Paris in 1886, after hearing from his brother Theo about the new, colourful style of French painting. Wasting no time, he tried it out in several self-portraits. He did this mostly to avoid having to pay for a model. Using rhythmic brushstrokes in striking colours, he portrayed himself here as a fashionably dressed Parisian.

challenges.title: Daily Art #11 — Forest path or lane

options.name: Avenue of Oaks in Late Summer
options.exhibitScript: Marie Bilders-van Bosse likely painted this picture in the vicinity of Oosterbeek, where she moved after her marriage. She was a member of the Hague School, a group of painters who focussed on capturing the atmosphere and light of nature. In this painting, three full-grown trees dominate the composition. It is only upon closer inspection that one notices the countrylane and the figure of a woman.

options.name: Landscape
options.artist: Jacob Jacobsz van Geel (signed by artist)
options.dimensions: support: height 49 cm x width 73.9 cm
options.exhibitScript: In a forest with majestic giant trees, a few tiny figures can be seen conversing. Their insignificance makes the trees, with their fanciful shapes and eccentric foliage, seem even larger. Van Geel stands apart from most Dutch landscape painters. He was not interested in realistic landscapes, but preferred imaginary ones.

options.name: Bos met berkebomen
options.exhibitScript: undefined

answer.name: Maria met kind
answer.dimensions: height 77 cm x width 57 cm x thickness 1.8 cm

options.name: Virgin and Child
options.artist: attributed to Meester van San Miniato
options.exhibitScript: undefined

options.name: Madonna and Child
options.exhibitScript:
The style of the Master of Badia a Isola is closely related to that of Duccio, the founder of the Sienese school of painting. This Virgin is a variation on a prototype by Duccio. Although the bottom of the panel has been cut off, it is still an excellent example of the refined linear
style that Duccio passed on to his followers in Siena.

options.name: Madonna and Child
options.exhibitScript: undefined

challenges.title: Daily Art #13 — Portrait, self-portrait of painter

options.name: Self Portrait
options.exhibitScript: Vincent moved to Paris in 1886, after hearing from his brother Theo about the new, colourful style of French painting. Wasting no time, he tried it out in several self-portraits. He did this mostly to avoid having to pay for a model. Using rhythmic brushstrokes in striking colours, he portrayed himself here as a fashionably dressed Parisian.

options.name: Self Portrait as the Apostle Paul
options.exhibitScript: Rembrandt is 55 years old in this self-portrait, and he still looks at himself candidly. Here he assumes the guise of a personage from the Bible, the Apostle Paul. The sword protruding from his cloak and the manuscript in his hands are the apostle’s customary attributes. By using his own face, Rembrandt encouraged the viewer to engage personally with the saint.

options.name: Portrait of Lizzy Ansingh (1875-1959)
options.exhibitScript: Thérèse Schwartze was the most sought-after portraitist of Dutch 19th-century high society. Yet the portraits she painted of those closest to her were her favourites. This likeness of her niece and fellow artist Lizzy Ansingh is one of her best works. The strong contrast between light and dark creates an intimate atmosphere. At the same time, the energetic brushwork and fiery red accent on the bright green in her clothes exude a powerful aura.

answer.name: Portret van Pieter Jacobsz. Schout, burgemeester van Haarlem
answer.dimensions: outer size: depth 8.5 cm (support incl. SK-L-2325)

options.name: Andries Bicker (1586-1652). Trader with Russia and Burgomaster of Amsterdam
options.exhibitScript: Andries Bicker, a merchant in Russia and dealer in spices, wielded enormous administrative power. He was elected mayor of Amsterdam ten times, represented the city in the States-General, and served as ambassador to the Scandinavian nations, Poland and the Duchy of Brandenburg. When he sat to the celebrated Amsterdam portraitist Van der Helst, the Bickers were the most powerful family in the city.

options.name: Portrait of Johannes Hudde (1628-1704), burgomaster of Amsterdam
options.exhibitScript: Michiel van Musscher, a pupil of Gabriel Metsu, painted this portrait with great precision. The sitter, Johannes Hudde, wears a morning gown made of Chinese silk, known in Dutch as a ‘Japanse rok.’ While it was de rigueur to wear this type of garment around the house, it was also likely meant to reference his role as a Dutch East India Company (VOC) administrator. The large book on the table with the most important rights or charters of Amsterdam makes it clear that Hudde was also a burgomaster of that city.

options.name: Mr. Pieter Cornelis Hasselaer (1720-96), raad van Indië en burgemeester van Amsterdam met zijn gezin
options.dimensions: height 248 cm x width 285 cm x thickness 4 cm
options.exhibitScript: undefined

answer.name: The Demolition of the Grand Bazar de la Bourse on the Nieuwendijk, Amsterdam

options.name: View of Haarlem from the northwest, with the bleaching fields in the foreground
options.exhibitScript: Foreigners experience the flat Dutch landscape as having a straight, low horizon extending under a vast sky with billowing cumulus clouds. This is how Ruisdael painted the Haarlem skyline in the distance, recognizable by the high roof of St Bavo’s. Lengths of cloth bleaching in the sun lie at the foot of the dunes in the foreground. The Haarlem linen industry relied on the pure dune water.

options.name: The Rokin, Amsterdam
options.exhibitScript: undefined

options.name: Pakhuizen aan de Teertuinen op het Prinseneiland te Amsterdam
options.exhibitScript: undefined

options.name: Titus in Monk's Habit
options.exhibitScript: Rembrandt portrayed his son Titus with downcast eyes and wearing a Franciscan habit. The rules of this monastic order prescribed a life of poverty and humility. Indicative of this are the coarse robe and the introspective gaze on the boy’s gaunt face, on which Rembrandt has focussed all his attention. The surroundings, with rocks and bushes, are only summarily suggested.

options.name: Giuliano & Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo Piero di Cosimo (1462–1522)
options.exhibitScript: In these pendant portraits, the Florentine architect Giuliano da Sangallo is portrayed with his recently deceased father Francesco, who was a musician as well as an architect. These two panels are among the earliest examples of portraits in which the sitter’s vocation is central. The quill and compass denote architecture, the sheet of notes refers to music: both arts are based on harmony and proportion.

options.name: Gezicht op Algiers met Michiel Adriaenszoon de Ruyters schip 'De Liefde', 1662
options.dimensions: support: height 65.5 cm x width 110 cm
options.exhibitScript: Four views of North-African cities. Reinier Nooms, c. 1665. To protect the Dutch merchant fleet, the navy took the fight the enemy, far beyond the country's coastal waters. The Mediterranean harbours shown here - Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Salee - were bases from which privateers attacked Dutch merchant shipping. Attempts were made to impose treaties on these cities using the threat of naval force. Between 1661 and 1663 Admiral Michiel de Ruyter cruised the Mediterranean Sea in an effort to stamp out piracy. De Ruyter's ship, de Liefde, is shown in the foreground of the view of Algiers. This series of paintings was displayed in one of the rooms of the Amsterdam Admiralty. The Republic's navy was divided into five regional admiralties, one of which was based in Amsterdam.

options.name: The Naval Battle near Livorno, 14 March 1653
options.exhibitScript: A painted sheet of paper at the lower left describes the subject: the battle between the Dutch and English fleets off the Italian port of Livorno (Leghorn). Victory brought the Dutch control over trade in the Mediterranean. Nooms almost certainly painted the canvas for the Chamber of Levantine Trade in Amsterdam.

options.name: Schepen op een onstuimige zee
options.exhibitScript: undefined

challenges.title: Daily Art #19 — Landscapes in the temperate zone

answer.name: Het onweer
answer.exhibitScript: The rapid, powerful brushwork of this landscape makes it look more like an oil sketch than a finished painting. As such, Tavenraat deviated from the path taken by most Dutch painters at the time. Instead of a smooth painting style and restrained sentiment, he opted for bright colours and dramatic effects of light and dark. His work thus more closely reflects international, mainly French, developments in Romantic painting.

options.name: Italian Landscape with Umbrella Pines
options.exhibitScript: Voogd was known as the ‘Dutch Claude’, after the French painter Claude Lorrain, who was famous for his history landscapes bathed in golden light. Voogd painted the gardens of the Villa Borghese in Rome in the late afternoon. The sun casts long shadows, and the trees stand out sharply against the sky. Strolling figures are enjoying the magnificent sunset. In the foreground, an artist is seated against a tree and drawing.

options.name: Portrait of a Couple in a Landscape, probably Isaac Abrahamsz Massa (1586-1643) and Beatrix van der Laen (1592-1639)
options.dimensions: support: height 140 cm x width 166.5 cm
options.exhibitScript: It was rare for a married couple to be portrayed lifesize and together in a single painting, and even more so in such an intimate and joyful manner. The patron would have had to have been receptive to such innovation. The close friendship between Hals and the newlywed couple, presumably Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, likely played a crucial role in facilitating the creation of this audacious portrait.

options.name: Portraits of Giuliano and Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo
options.exhibitScript: This exceptional diptych shows the leading Florentine architect Giuliano da Sangallo and his recently deceased father Francesco, who was an architect as well as a musician. These are among the earliest portraits to characterize their sitters by profession: a pen and compass allude to architecture, the sheet music to music. Both disciplines are based on notions of harmony and proportion.

answer.name: The Archangel Gabriel Appearing to Zacharias
answer.dimensions: outer size: depth 6 cm (support incl. frame)

options.name: Het koor van de Nieuwe Kerk te Amsterdam met het praalgraf van Michiel Adriaenszoon de Ruyter
options.exhibitScript: Visitors are admiring Admiral de Ruyter’s imposing tomb monument. It occupies the place where the main altar once stood when the Nieuwe Kerk was still a Catholic church. The monumental marble tomb is a tribute to the naval hero, who died in 1667 off the coast of Sicily at the age of 69. It was designed by the sculptor Rombout Verhulst. De Ruyter’s son commissioned this painting.

options.name: The Holy Kinship
options.artist: workshop of Geertgen tot Sint Jans
options.dimensions: support: height 137.2 cm x width 105.8 cm
options.exhibitScript: Christ’s next of kin are gathered in an imaginary medieval church. The term for such a scene derives from the word for a blood relation. At left, the Virgin (in blue) is seated with Jesus on her lap beside her mother, Anne. Behind them stand their husbands, Joachim and Joseph. At right sits Mary’s cousin Elizabeth with her son, John the Baptist.

options.name: The Inauguration of King William II in the Nieuwe Kerk in Amsterdam on 28 November 1840
options.exhibitScript: Investiture of King William II in the New Church in Amsterdam on 28 November 1840, by N. Pieneman. Sketch for a large painting produced by Pieneman for the Royal House. To the left of the king: his brother Frederick and his son Alexander. To the right: his sons William and Henry. In the background, centre: Queen Anna Paulowna with, left, her daughter-in-law Sophie (the wife of Crown Prince William),and right, her daughter-in-law Louise of Prussia (wife of Prince Frederick) and her daughter Sophie. On the table in the centre are a copy of the constitution, the crown, the orb and sceptre. Like the sword of state and the national standard these were especially made for the occasion.
